narrative:
As a white male applicant with a middle-income family background, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on profiles of past students. The model looked at key aspects of your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.70—which is below the typical 3.21 average—and your LSAT score of 25, significantly lower than the average of 36.70. It also considered your placement in the lowest decile for grades in both your first and third years of school, far below the middle-of-the-pack averages around the 5th or 6th decile. Your plan to attend full-time, which is the norm for over 90% of students, didn't sway things much either.

The model predicted a 96% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is why your application was rejected—law schools use this to prioritize applicants likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly.

This high failure probability stemmed primarily from three key factors in your profile, ranked by their influence on the prediction.

The strongest driver was your third-year school decile ranking at the bottom (1st decile), which strongly pushed the model toward predicting bar failure because it signals consistently low performance compared to peers.

Next, your low LSAT score had a notable push in the same direction, as scores like yours are linked to struggles on high-stakes exams like the bar.

Your first-year decile at the bottom also contributed toward the failure prediction, reinforcing a pattern of underperformance from early on.

Overall, your academic metrics placed you well below typical successful students, tipping the scales decisively against admission.

To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your grades in any ongoing studies to climb those decile rankings, and perhaps exploring targeted bar prep resources or community college courses to build a stronger track record. With focused improvements, you could reapply stronger next cycle and show the model—and admissions teams—a turnaround story tailored to your circumstances.
